question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a sidecar record with a ContractBytecode whose contractId encodes a system entity ID (e.g., treasury account 0.0.2), causing updateContractType() to change the type of a system entity to CONTRACT in the entity table, corrupting the historical record of system account types?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that updateContractType() uses 'type <> CONTRACT' as its only guard — with no validation that the target entity actually has contract bytecode or was created via a ContractCreate transaction — to cause the entity type of any ACCOUNT or TOKEN entity to be permanently overwritten to CONTRACT, falsifying the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a sidecar record stream item with a very large list of ContractBytecode entries (approaching IN_CLAUSE_LIMIT = 32767) containing IDs of non-contract entities, causing updateContractType() to bulk-update thousands of entity type records to CONTRACT in a single transaction, causing a mass corruption of the Hashgraph entity type history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user cause updateContractType() to be called with entity IDs that do not exist in the entity table, and if the UPDATE silently affects zero rows without raising an error, cause the importer to proceed as if the migration succeeded, leaving the entity_history table in an inconsistent state where some historical records have the wrong type?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a sidecar record that causes updateContractType() to be called on an entity that is currently being processed in the same record file batch, creating a race condition where the entity's type is updated to CONTRACT before the entity listener has finished processing the entity's creation transaction, resulting in an incorrect type being stored in entity_history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a sidecar record where the ContractBytecode's contractId uses a non-zero shard or realm, causing EntityId.of(contractBytecode.getContractId()).getId() to compute an entity ID that collides with a legitimate entity in a different shard/realm, and updateContractType() to corrupt that entity's type in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Modifying annotation on updateContractType() — which bypasses the JPA first-level cache — to cause a situation where the in-memory entity state held by the EntityManager differs from the database state after the bulk update, leading to subsequent save() calls overwriting the CONTRACT type with the old type and corrupting the entity history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a sidecar record that triggers updateContractType() on an entity whose type is SCHEDULE or FILE, causing the entity type to be permanently changed to CONTRACT in the entity table, while the entity_history table retains the original type, creating an inconsistency between current and historical state that falsifies the Hashgraph record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted entity object via the record stream that causes CrudRepository.save() to overwrite an existing entity's createdTimestamp (which is marked @Column(updatable = false) in AbstractEntity) through a JPA merge operation, corrupting the historical creation timestamp of the entity in the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes the importer to call save() on an entity with a timestamp_range that overlaps with an existing entity_history record, corrupting the temporal integrity of the entity history and causing historical queries to return incorrect state for a given block timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item that causes save() to be called with an entity whose alias field is set to a value that was previously used by a deleted entity, causing the entity table to have two historical records (one in entity_history, one current) with the same alias, breaking the alias uniqueness invariant and corrupting alias-based historical lookups?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes save() to be called with an entity whose evmAddress is set to the same value as another active entity's evmAddress, causing findByEvmAddress() to return an ambiguous result and corrupting the EVM address resolution history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item that causes save() to be called with a negative or zero entity ID, and if the database does not enforce a positive ID constraint, cause a phantom entity to be inserted into the entity table that interferes with legitimate entity lookups and corrupts the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes save() to be called with an entity whose balance field is set to a negative value, exploiting the UpsertColumn coalesce logic for balance (which adds existing and new balance) to cause the entity's balance to underflow, corrupting the historical balance record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item that causes save() to be called with an entity whose ethereumNonce is set to a very large value, exploiting the UpsertColumn coalesce logic for ethereumNonce (which adds existing and new nonce) to cause an integer overflow in the nonce field, corrupting the historical nonce record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.save(Entity)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes save() to be called with an entity whose memo field contains a null byte (char 0), and if the database does not sanitize null bytes, cause the memo to be truncated or corrupted in the entity table, falsifying the historical memo record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.deleteById(Long)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes the importer to call deleteById() on an entity that should only be soft-deleted (deleted=true), causing the entity to be permanently removed from the entity table without a corresponding entry in entity_history, creating a gap in the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: CrudRepository.findAll()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the importer to call findAll() on the entity table during a high-volume record stream ingestion, triggering a full table scan that causes a denial-of-service condition, preventing the importer from processing new record files and creating a gap in the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item with an alias that is exactly 20 bytes long (EVM_ADDRESS_LENGTH), causing EntityIdServiceImpl to route the lookup to findByEvmAddress() instead of findByAlias(), and if the entity was registered with an alias (not an EVM address), cause the lookup to fail and the transaction to be associated with EntityId.EMPTY, corrupting the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityIdServiceImpl cache (which caches results of findByAlias()) to cause a cache poisoning attack where a valid alias lookup result is cached, then the entity is deleted and a new entity is created with the same alias, causing all subsequent lookups to return the old (deleted) entity ID from cache, corrupting the historical entity resolution?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item where the AccountID alias field contains bytes that are valid as both an ECDSA secp256k1 public key alias and a raw EVM address, causing EntityIdServiceImpl to attempt both findByAlias() and findByAliasEvmAddress() lookups and potentially resolve to two different entity IDs, corrupting the historical transaction attribution?
